macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Mishnah's Case of Unknown Marriage

micro_segments.title: Opening of the daf and context of the Mishnah
The case of marrying one of two sisters without knowing which

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Does Kiddushin She'einam Re'uyim LeB'iah Bind?

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's inference that kiddushin never possible to lead to sex is still binding

macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Mishnah's Case of Unknown Marriage (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rashi's explanation of how the marriage could be indeterminate from the outset

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Does Kiddushin She'einam Re'uyim LeB'iah Bind? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Charlie's question about kiddushin with arayos versus lo ta'aseh prohibitions
Tosafot's distinction that the kiddushin itself creates the impossibility
The Gemara's resolution: the Mishnah discusses a case where it was known at the outset

macro_segments.title: The Significance of the Mishnah's Language and the Seifa

micro_segments.title: Analysis of the Mishnah's language: veino yodeia vs. veino yadu'a
The seifa's role in teaching the practical halacha of chalitzah and yibum
Example with one brother: he must do chalitzah to both women
Example with two brothers: order of chalitzah then yibum

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two Unrelated Men Marrying Two Sisters

micro_segments.title: Scenario: two unrelated men (one with one brother, one with two) marry two sisters unknowingly
When one man dies with one brother: the brother does chalitzah to both
When both men die with different numbers of brothers: chalitzah and yibum distribution
Why the single brother cannot do yibum first: the problem of achot zikukato

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two Brothers Marrying Two Sisters with Uncertainty

micro_segments.title: Scenario: two brothers each with two brothers marry two sisters unknowingly
Solution: each pair does chalitzah with one woman, yibum with the other
Why this removes the problem of achot zikukato through dual actions

macro_segments.title: Distinction Between the Unknown Case and the Certain Case of Two Sisters

micro_segments.title: The mishnah's case of Reuven and Shimon married to Rachel and Leah, both now dead
Why both must do chalitzah: achot zikukato becomes achot asurah permanently
Rashi's explanation of achot zikukato becoming asurah as eshet ach
The danger of bitul mitzvas yevamim if yibum were allowed
Why the uncertain case tolerates yibum by both: only one was obligated

macro_segments.title: If Both Brothers Perform Yibum: The Case of Two Priests

micro_segments.title: If both brothers perform yibum, they are allowed to remain married
New question: what if both brothers are kohanim? Can they stay married to chalutza?
Chalutza's status is only rabbinic, not d'oraisa
Since one is only a safek chalutza, they can remain married
Debate: is chalutza d'oraisa or d'rabanan? The Bavli's position

macro_segments.title: The Mitzvah of Yibum: Preference for the Older Brother

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: the mitzvah of yibum is with the gadol (older brother)
If the younger brother rushes ahead and performs yibum, it is valid

macro_segments.title: Drashah from Vehaya Habechor: Does Only the Bechor Perform Yibum?

micro_segments.title: Reading the verse vehaya habechor: the bechor does yibum with a fertile woman
Asher teleid: the woman must be capable of bearing children (not ilonit)
Yakum al shem achivu lenachala: the brother inherits the dead brother's portion
Velo yimache shemo miYisrael: the name continues because the brothers remain

macro_segments.title: Rava's Observation: The Verse Has No Literal Peshat Meaning

micro_segments.title: Rava: although in all of Torah, drashah preserves some peshat value, here it does not
The gezeirah shavah between al shem achivu and lenachala completely removes peshat
The peshat of naming the son after the father is completely eradicated

macro_segments.title: Challenges to the Interpretation: To Whom Is the Verse Speaking?

micro_segments.title: The verse should address the yavam directly if it means naming the child
Alternative reading: the verse addresses the Beit Din and Klal Yisrael generally
Why the verse says achiv and not abiv: interpreting the brother reference

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Resolution: The Verse Is About the Brother, Not the Son

micro_segments.title: The verse addresses the Beit Din: the yavam must ensure the firstborn is named for the dead brother's inheritance
All references in the verse concern the brother performing yibum, not the child born

macro_segments.title: Does Only the Bechor Perform Yibum? Testing the Theory

micro_segments.title: Concern: maybe only a bechor (firstborn son) can perform yibum, non-firstborns cannot
Eshet achivu shelo haya beolamo proves younger brothers can perform yibum

macro_segments.title: Can Yibum Be Performed If There Is No Bechor Alive?

micro_segments.title: When brothers sit together: the verse links all brothers together

macro_segments.title: Does Only the Bechor Perform Yibum? Testing the Theory (continued)

micro_segments.title: The brother born after his brother died cannot be a bechor, yet yibum applies

macro_segments.title: Can Yibum Be Performed If There Is No Bechor Alive? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Concern: maybe yibum only works when a bechor is alive
Veyom v'ach scenario confirms yibum can occur with only younger brother remaining

macro_segments.title: Understanding 'Gadol' and the Hierarchy of Brothers

micro_segments.title: Abbaye teaches: mitzvah for the gadol to perform yibum
If gadol refuses, they turn to the next younger brother (achiv hakatan)

macro_segments.title: Does Preference for the Bechor Negate Yibum by Younger Brothers?

micro_segments.title: Even if bechor has preference, can a younger brother still perform yibum?
Concern: maybe if there is a bechor, younger brothers cannot perform yibum
The mishnah's language 'gadol' not 'bechor' suggests preference not exclusivity

macro_segments.title: Understanding 'Gadol' and the Hierarchy of Brothers (continued)

micro_segments.title: If all refuse, demand returns to the gadol for chalitzah or yibum
The verse uses bechor but means gadol: ke-bechor, just as firstborn status prioritizes, so does age

macro_segments.title: Does Only the Bechor Inherit the Dead Brother's Portion?

micro_segments.title: Concern: maybe inheritance of dead brother's portion is only for the bechor
Yakum al shem achivu applies to whoever performs yibum, not just the bechor

macro_segments.title: The Bechor's Status and Its Limits in Yibum Context

micro_segments.title: Why use the term bechor if not only the bechor performs yibum?
Legeriyuta: bechor status is mentioned to limit its privileges
The bechor's double portion applies only to actual possession, not prospective property
The yavam who performs yibum only inherits dead brother's actual possessions

macro_segments.title: Example: Inheritance Distribution With Yibum and Father's Death

micro_segments.title: Scenario: father has 1 million in possession, 100K in outstanding loans; Reuven dies
Shimeon performs yibum; he gets Reuven's portion of the 1 million only
The 100K debt is divided equally among all heirs, not including Reuven's double share
Girsa from Gra citing Rabbeinu Tam: different understanding of the passage

macro_segments.title: The Five Brothers and the Collected Debt: Mishnah on Daf Kaf-Dalet Amud Aleph

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: five brothers collect a debt owed to their father
The bechor does not take a double portion in the debt
The reason: the debt is rau'i not muchzak—it was not in the father's possession
If they collect land instead of money, the status of the land remains rau'i
Mach loket in Gemara: some hold land becomes muchzak and bechor gets double

topical_tags.term: Rau'i vs. Muchzak (Prospective vs. Actual Possession)
